This is a sample transcript for pipeline testing. It contains content across all three domains.

[00:00] Welcome everyone to this week's community call. Let me share my screen.

[00:30] Today we're going to talk about three things: our new deployment process using GitHub Actions,
some exciting work we've been doing with RAG pipelines for one of our clients, and then Jack
is going to share some updates on the business development side.

[02:00] So first up, deployment. We've moved everything over to GitHub Actions. The key thing
here is we have a staging environment that runs on every PR merge, and production deploys
only happen when you tag a release. We use environment secrets for all API keys.

[05:00] We also added pre-commit hooks for formatting and linting. The team was spending too
much time in code review on style issues, so this automates that completely. If the hooks
fail, the commit doesn't happen.

[10:00] Now let me talk about the RAG work. One of our clients needed to search across 50,000
internal documents. We built a retrieval system using pgvector with a hybrid search approach —
combining BM25 keyword search with vector similarity and then using reciprocal rank fusion
to merge the results.

[15:00] The key architectural decision was whether to use a hosted vector database or self-host.
We went with self-hosted pgvector because the client already had PostgreSQL, and adding
vector support was just enabling an extension. The total cost increase was basically zero.

[20:00] For the embedding model, we used nomic-embed-text running locally on Ollama. 768 dimensions,
very fast, and the quality was excellent for this use case. The critical rule we enforced was
one embedding model everywhere — never mix models or your similarity scores become meaningless.

[28:00] Alright, Jack on the business side.

[28:30] Thanks. So we closed two new deals this quarter, both AI-adjacent. The first is a
traditional custom software project — a logistics company in Houston needs a warehouse
management system. Good revenue, straightforward scope.

[32:00] The second deal is more interesting. A Dallas law firm wants us to build an AI document
review tool. They're uploading contracts and want the system to flag unusual clauses and
summarize key terms. This is squarely in our new AI solutions practice.

[36:00] For pricing strategy on AI projects, we've been moving toward value-based pricing
rather than hourly rates. The law firm deal is priced at $85,000 fixed, which would have
been $55,000 under our old hourly model. The client is happy because they know the total cost.

[40:00] One thing I want everyone to understand is that these AI projects have a much higher
perceived value to clients than traditional software. A RAG system that saves 20 hours a week
of manual document review is worth hundreds of thousands of dollars per year to them.

[45:00] Questions from the team?

[45:30] Can we use Kimi K2 instead of DeepSeek for the report generation? It tends to give
better structured output in my experience.

[46:00] Yes, the model routing is all config-based, so you can switch by changing an environment
variable. Never hardcode the model name in the code.

[48:00] That's all for today. Next week we'll do a deep dive on the agent orchestration
patterns we've been using with LangGraph. See everyone then.
